coredata.dc:title: A sensitive electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures for determination of cytostatic drug flutamide
coredata.dc:creator: Modarres Zahed, Fatemeh
Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Kalate Bojdi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Using an electrochemical sensor with high sensing capability and manufacturing simplicity, flutamide (FLU) can be quantitatively measured. A glassy carbon electrode is modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures (HFGNs). Combining GQDs and HFGNs enhanced the electrode surface and electron transfer rate, improving the signal's intensity and sensitivity. Nanostructure characterization, electrochemical characterization, and detection studies indicated the high performance of the platform in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and stability. With these improvements, linear correlations were achieved between 0.01 and 400 µM, with a detection limit of 6.2 nM (based on 3Ssb/m). Pharmaceuticals and complex biological samples along with interfering agents have been successfully analyzed with this platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flutamide
Graphene quantum dots
Hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity of the sensor to FLU

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) size distribution of GQDs, (C) and (D) FESEM images of HFGNs in different magnitudes; 2 μm and 1 μm, respectively. (E) EDX analysis of HFGNs/GQDs/GCE.

(A) CVs and (B) EIS of unmodified and modified electrodes in solution of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. (C) DPVs of unmodified and modified electrodes in the presence of 10.0 μM FLU in BR (pH 7.0).
(A) DPVs of 10 μM FLU in BR buffer electrolyte solution at different pHs (4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0, and 10), (B) Relationship between current and potential of FLU reduction peak vs. pH.
(A) CVs of 30.0 μM FLU in BR (pH 6.0) at different scan rate (20–250 mV/s). (B) Plot of Ip vs. scan rate. (C) Plot of Ep vs. log v.

DPV responses of the developed sensor toward FLU with different concentrations (A). Calibration graphs of FLU at surface of HFGNs/GQDs/GCE in the concentrations of 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 µM (B), and in the concentrations of 1.0, 10, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 µM (C) (n = 3).

The selectivity of the HFGNs/GQDs/GCE toward FLU (10 µM) after additions of (A) Several drugs (100 μM of nilutamide, chloramphenicol, metronidazole, doxycycline, 4-nitrophenol, and paracetamol), (B) Biomolecules and ions (500 μM of glucose, sucrose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, and citric acid and 1000 µM of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-). (C) The stability of the sensor for 5 weeks.
Comparison of this study with some other FLU electrochemical sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Hexagonal boron nitride decorated on bismuth oxide modified screen-printed carbon electrode.
Lanthanum doped zirconia nanoparticles into graphene quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of quantitative analyze of FLU in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % Recovery = (amount of FLU determined/amount of FLU taken) × 100.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD (%) for three sets of LOD data.

head.ce:title: A sensitive electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures for determination of cytostatic drug flutamide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Birjand University, P. O. Box 97176-615, Birjand, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Birjand University, P. O. Box 97176-615, Birjand, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Antimicrobial Resistance Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Antimicrobial Resistance Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using an electrochemical sensor with high sensing capability and manufacturing simplicity, flutamide (FLU) can be quantitatively measured. A glassy carbon electrode is modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures (HFGNs). Combining GQDs and HFGNs enhanced the electrode surface and electron transfer rate, improving the signal's intensity and sensitivity. Nanostructure characterization, electrochemical characterization, and detection studies indicated the high performance of the platform in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and stability. With these improvements, linear correlations were achieved between 0.01 and 400 µM, with a detection limit of 6.2 nM (based on 3Ssb/m). Pharmaceuticals and complex biological samples along with interfering agents have been successfully analyzed with this platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer can be treated with flutamide (FLU), a non-steroidal anti-androgen compound. As a result of testosterone, prostate cells expand in the body. Flu exhibits anti-androgen activity by blocking testosterone or inhibiting androgen binding to target tissues and preventing prostate cancer cell growth [1]. Women with the polycystic ovarian syndrome can also benefit from flu treatment [2]. After the treatment, FLU is excreted from the body in the form of urine, and a small amount (4.2 %) of it is metabolized and excreted unchanged through feces. Its hydroxylated metabolites are 3-trifluoromethyl-4-nitroaniline and 2-hydroxyflutamide [3]. The abnormal and long-term use of this medication can lead to numerous side effects in the body [4]. There are a number of side effects associated with this drug, including anemia, blurred vision, liver damage, a feeling of imbalance, depression, headache, jaundice, blood in the urine, breast fullness, nausea, etc. The determination of FLU in pharmaceuticals and biological samples is therefore of the utmost importance. FLU has been measured using a variety of methods, including photochemistry, spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, high-performance liquid chromatography, polarography, and electrochemical sensing [5–10]. There are a number of advantages associated with electrochemical techniques, including low price requirements, simple fabrication, high sensitivity, and rapidity [11]. In analytical chemistry and medicine, electrochemical techniques based on modified electrodes have become a serious diagnostic and research tool due to their significant effect on increasing sensor performance [12,13]. As electrode modifiers, nanomaterial have recently attracted considerable interest due to their unique properties [14]. It has been found that nanoscale materials exhibit exceptional properties such as high chemical stability, high conductivity, and excellent biocompatibility [15]. Therefore, it is not surprising that nanostructures are now being used to construct advanced electrochemical sensors. It is often difficult, costly, and time-consuming to synthesize some nanostructures. Additionally, some of the methods that are used to stabilize nanostructures on electrode surfaces require expensive reagents and specialized equipment and can be laborious [16,17].

The FLU electrochemical determination has basically been carried out using electrodes decorated with different nanostructures since on nanostructure-modified sensing platforms the electrochemical response is improved. Studies have reported the use of ionic liquid-mediated hollow fiber-pencil graphite electrode modified by graphene oxide functionalized with highly branched polyglycerol [18], a glassy carbon electrode modified by lanthanumdoped zirconia nanoparticles intographene quantum dots [19], a screen-printed carbon electrode modified by hexagonal-h boron nitride decorated on bismuth oxide [4], a glassy carbon electrode modified by lanthanum cobaltite decorated halloysite nanotube [20]], etc. [21–23], for determination of FLU in serum, urine, and environmental samples. Although these studies claim high sensitivity and selectivity, they were often labor-intensive, cautious, and time-consuming. Therefore, it is important to use a simple, cheap, and efficient method to synthesize nanostructures and modify the electrode surface.

Due to their high conductivity and large surface area, carbon nanomaterial such as carbon dots, graphene, and carbon nanotubes are widely used to modify electrode surfaces [24]. One of the most interesting members of the carbon nanomaterial family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), possesses both the unique properties of graphene and the excellent properties of carbon dots. The compound has certain characteristics such as low cytotoxicity, simple synthesis, good conductivity, excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, abundant edge sites, and high stability [25–27].

Due to the characteristic features of gold nanoparticles, such as their high stability, excellent conductivity, and favorable biocompatibility, gold nanoparticles have been considered for fabricating electrochemical sensors [28].

A sensor's performance is greatly influenced by the morphology and the size of gold nanoparticles. Various methods have been used to synthesize gold nanostructures, including rods, cubes, flowers, wires, dendrites, and stars [29]. In the field of constructing electrochemical sensors, hierarchical flower-like gold nanostructures (HFGNs) have attracted much research attention due to their specific forms, easy preparation, excellent electrochemical properties, and high surface area [30].

The above findings led to the development of a sensitive platform based on GQDs and HFGNs. In order to accomplish this, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface was first decorated with GQDs. We have successfully synthesized shape-controlled HFGNs on GQDs/GCE using an electrodeposition method. As a result of this electrode surface modification strategy, the analysis of the electrode was quick and easy, saving both time and reagents. The constructed sensor was utilized for quantitative analysis of FLU in biological samples and pharmaceutical compounds with high accuracy (relative recovery between 98.14 % − 102.11 % and RSD < 3.06).

Flutamide and HAuCl4·3H2O were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. KCl, ethylene diamine, glucose, HCl, H3BO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, NaOH, K3[Fe(CN)6], and K4[Fe(CN)6] were purchased from Merck. For electrochemical analysis, a potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab PGSTAT 101) was used. The three-electrode system consists of a working electrode (GCE), an auxiliary electrode (platinum electrode), and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl with saturated KCl). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDS) were recorded using a TESCAN MIRA3-FEG. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were taken using ALEO 912 AB TEM.

In order to perform a simple and easy hydrothermal synthesis of GQD, Gu et al.'s research was used [31]. Briefly, a mixture containing 800 µL ethylene diamine, 400 μL HCl (37 %, w/w), and 2.0 g glucose was prepared in 30 mL deionized water. After stirring for 20 min, the mixed solution was autoclaved. It was then placed in the oven at 200 degrees for six hours to perform the hydrothermal process. In the centrifuge bed, the solution was centrifuged for 30 min at 6000 rpm once the solution had turned brown. At the end of the process, GQDs were synthesized and stored at 4 ˚C.

It is typically polished with alumina powder, rinsed with ultra-purified water, and then sonicated for 2 min a water: ethanol (50:50, v/v) solution to ensure that the GCE surface is clean and free from disturbances. The electrode surface was modified by first dropping 5 L of GQDs on top of the GCE and then drying at ambient temperature. GQDs/GCE was then immersed in the HAuCl4 solution (0.2 mM, prepared with 0.1 M KCl) and chronoamperometrically charged for 1800 s to form HFGNs.

A solution of 1.0 × 10-3 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl was used to conduct cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. Using a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, CV voltammograms were obtained by scanning from 0.2 V to 0.5 V. The amplitude of the EIS studies was set at 10 mV and the frequency range was 10–100 kHz. FLU electrochemical behavior was investigated by measuring CVs in a solution of 30 M FLU by scanning between 0.8 and 0.0 V. In the first oxidative scan a significant anodic peak was identified at −0.19 V, while in the reverse scan, two cathodic peaks were observed at −0.21 and −0.62 V. Two distinct redox peaks at −0.19 V and –0.21 V, which are probably due to arylhydroxylamine/nitrosophenol and a very sharp reduction peak at −0.62 V corresponding to the direct reduction of the –NO2 group to arylhydroxylamine [32–34]. In electrochemical studies, the cathodic peak plays a major role. Therefore, all experimental measures (such as pH and scan rate) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were conducted based on their potential and current. For the FLU determination, DPV measurements were performed from −0.9 to −0.3 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s-1, modulation time of 25 mV, modulation amplitude of 0.05 s, and step potential of 5 mV.

TEM, FESEM, and EDS techniques were used to investigate the characteristics of the synthesized nanostructures. In Fig. 1
A, the TEM image of GQDs revealed a uniform distribution of spherical particles with a diameter estimated to be around 5 nm based on the histogram of the GQDs ( Fig. 1 B). As illustrated in Fig. 1 C and D with 2 μm and 1 μm magnification, gold nanostructures were successfully assembled on the surface of GQDs/GCE by the electrosynthesis procedure. Based on the FESEM images, it appears that the synthesized gold nanostructures have a hierarchical flower-like structure [30].
EDS analysis was also performed on the modified electrode to determine the constituent elements. Fig. 1 E displays the existence of C, O, and Au in the EDX analysis of HFGNs/GQDs/GCE.

Two steps of the surface modification of GCE were electrochemically characterized using CV, EIS, and DPV techniques. Using 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 as a redox probe, CV voltammograms and EIS nyquist plots were observed in 0.1 M KCl solution. As shown in Fig. 2
A and B, GQDs/GCE exhibit significantly higher CV response than bare GCE, and their electron transfer resistance (Ret) is significantly lower than bare GCE due to their excellent electroconductive properties [35]. In the following, by electrodeposition of HFGNs at the surface of GQDs/GCE, due to expansion of the active surface area of the electrode and improvement of the conductivity, the CV peak currents are increased and Ret decreased. Electron transfer between electrodes and electrolyte solutions increased significantly when GQDs and HFGNs were used in comparison with other electrode materials.

Additionally, FLU electrochemical behavior on unmodified and modified electrodes was studied in BR (pH 7.0) when formulated with 10.0 μM FLU. Due to their high surface area and good electro-conductivity, GQDs/GCE and HFGNs/GCE showed a high current response as compared to bare GCE DPV ( Fig. 2 C). For the HFGNs/GQDs/GCE, the FLU peak became more pronounced, due to synergistic effects between GQD and HFGNs. The HFGNs/GQDs nanocomposite is suitable for use as an electrode for FLU sensing, based on these observations.

To make the sensor show maximum performance, the effect of pH on peak current and corresponding peak potential of 10 µM FLU was investigated by DPV measurements in the potential range of −1.0 to −0.2 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 using BR buffers with different pH values (4.0–10). As proclaimed in Fig. 3
A, when pH is increased from 4.0 to 7.0, the peak current of the FLU increases rapidly and its potential is shifted to the negative side. However, when the pH value increased from 7.0 to 10, the FLU peak current dropped. Therefore, the optimal pH value was set at 7.0. In addition, a good relationship was established between peak potential and pH of the supporting electrolyte as follow ( Fig. 3 B):
(1)
E p
V
= - 0.054 p H - 0.2554 ;
R
2
= 0.9968

The slope of −0.054 V/pH obtained here is close to the Nernst theoretical value (0.059 V/pH) [36], confirming that an equal number of electrons and protons participated in the electron transfer reaction of FLU at the surface of HFGNs/GQDs/GCE.

In general, the relationship between scan rate, peak potential, and peak current provides valuable information regarding electrode processes. For this, CV measurements were done in a solution of 30 µM FLU prepared in BR buffer (pH 7.0) at different scan rates (20 to 250 mV s−1). As shown in Fig. 4
A, the cathodic peak current increased with an increasing scan rate. According to a linear relation between the cathodic peak current and the scan rate ( Fig. 4 B), FLU's redox reaction is a typical surface-controlled process indicating fast electron transfer kinetics for HFGNs/GQDs/GCE [3,4,19]. As the scan rate increased, a slight shift in the reduction peak potential was observed, representing the irreversible nature of the electrode reaction of FLU on HFGNs/GQDs/GCE. The linear regression equation of peak potential versus log υ can be expressed by ( Fig. 4 C):
(2)
E
V
= 0.031
l o g
υ
V s
- 1
- 0.643 ;
R
2
= 0.9941

According to the Laviron model and from the slope of this dependence (2.3RT/αnF), αn value for the FLU reduction process was found to be 1.90. For an irreversible electrode reaction, the number of electrons involved in reducing FLU was estimated to be 3.81, which is in agreement with previous studies [2,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the sensor to FLU
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the optimized conditions, the constructed sensor was used to quantify FLU. Fig. 5
A displays the DP voltammograms at different concentrations of FLU in BR buffer (pH 7.0). As the concentration of FLU increased, the intensity of the FLU peak current increased tandemly. The calibration curve of peak current intensities vs. FLU concentration showed two linear ranges from 0.01 to 1.0 µM and from 1.0 to 400 µM ( Fig. 5 B and C). The equations of the first and second linear ranges were as follows:
(3)
I p
μ A
= - 13.30
C
F L U
μ M
- 9.610 ;
R
2
= 0.9950
(4)
I p
μ A
= - 0.0930
C
F L U
μ M
- 25.20 ;
R
2
= 0.9966
The limit of detection (LOD) value was obtained to be 6.2 nM based on the “LOD = 3 Ssb/m”.

An overview of the main features of previously reported FLU sensors can be found in Table 1 . According to the table, nanostructure materials play a crucial role in the detection of FLU. Generally, researchers prefer simple surface modification procedures when investigating these electrochemical sensors. This highlights the significance of the ease of the modification process in the design of electrochemical nanosensors. While the LOD of the proposed sensor was slightly higher than that of some sensors, the nanoparticles used in those studies were synthesized in a complex and expensive manner. This study used very simple and easy techniques to modify the electrode surface. It is anticipated that the developed electrochemical sensor will prove useful in anticancer drug assays in this context.

An assessment of the discrimination ability of the designed sensor toward FLU was conducted by comparing its peak current reduction in the presence of different interfering agents. For this several drugs including nilutamide, chloramphenicol, metronidazole, doxycycline, 4-nitrophenol, and paracetamol as well as several important metal ions and biologically related substances (glucose, sucrose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, and citric acid) were chosen as interfering matters. As displayed in Fig. 6
A and B, after the addition of 10-fold concentrations of drugs, 100-fold concentrations of ions, and 50-fold concentrations of biological compounds, the FLU reduction peak current changed slightly (less than 8 %) compared to the 10 µM FLU reduction peak current alone. The proposed sensor has good selectivity for FLU based on the results of these tests.

Under the same optimal conditions, six individual sensors were prepared to examine the reproducibility profile of the prepared sensor. These independent fabricated sensors used to measure 10 µM FLU in parallel. The relative standard deviation (RSD) value was obtained to be as 3.07 %, demonstrating the good reproducibility of the sensor.

Furthermore, the stability of the sensor was studied every five days over five weeks. For this, the electrode was kept in a refrigerator (4 ˚C) and every five days its signal was recorded in 10 µM FLU solution. As shown in Fig. 6 C, at the end of the 35 days storage, 94.6 % of its original response was retained.
Using the standard addition method, the fabricated electrochemical sensor was used to detect FLU in pharmaceutical formulations, urine and serum samples.

The first step in preparing pharmaceutical samples is to weigh five tablets to determine their average weights. Powdered tablets were then weighed after they had been powdered. According to the manufacturer's claim, the amount of active material in the weighed powder was calculated and solved in ethanol through sonication for 20 min. A pharmaceutical sample solution was prepared by filtering this solution and diluting it with BR buffer.

To prepare urine and serum samples, 0.5 mL of these samples (were taken from healthy volunteers) were spiked with different concentrations of FLU standard solutions (0.5 mL). To deproteinize of samples, 0.5 mL acetonitrile was added and precipitated proteins were separated by centrifugation. Finally, BR buffer was used to dilute the supernatants into working concentrations. The presented results in Table 2 indicate that the linear regression equations, recoveries, LODs and correlation coefficients (R2) in real sample matrices were consistent with that obtained in an aqueous standard solution. According to these observations, the fabricated sensor can be used to quantitatively analyze FLU in biological and pharmaceutical samples with satisfactory accuracy and sensitivity.

HFGNs/GQDs nanostructures were successfully used to construct a sensitive electrochemical sensor for quantifying FLU. As a result of the combination of GQDs and HFGNs, electrocatalytic activity toward FLU was enhanced. The fabricated sensor characterized by FESEM, EDS, CV, DPV, and EIS. The obtained results showed that the proposed sensor possessed excellent conductivity and high active area, improving the efficiency of the analytical signal. DPV measured FLU with good sensitivity, with linear ranges from 0.01 µm to 1.0 µm and 1.0 µm to 400 µm with a detection limit of 6.2 nM. The proposed sensor also displayed excellent selectivity, stability, and reproducibility. Importantly, HFGNs/GQDs also demonstrated excellent performance in the analysis of FLU in pharmaceutical formulations, urine, and serum samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2023.117109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
